story:
I was thirty-three and still felt like a collage—lines borrowed from novels, a magazine sentence here, a quote there—stitched together into something that never felt like mine. Dad, shrinking under the weight of cancer but still stubborn, stopped asking about boyfriends and started asking about the book, as if my draft were a child I was raising for him. I lied about my progress, carried guilt that ate at me, and stared at a Word document that blinked back like a half-formed thought. Inspiration wasn't a block; it was a barricade.

The night he flew home, I drove him to the airport, then went to a writing club I'd found on Reddit—eleven p.m. at a corner café. The time was odd, but I needed something. I found three people in red hoodies outside: Braxton, all elbows and typing fingers; Valerie, precise and distant behind round glasses; Adrian, soft-eyed and ripped-jeans casual. They didn't trade prompts or workshop pages. Valerie and Adrian stared through the crowd, murmuring lives as if they were reading captions under faces. Braxton hammered it all into his laptop.

A man who burned down a house as a kid and grew up to be a firefighter. A divorced father whose ex ruined his new wife's life at a funeral. I asked what book they were "reading." Valerie looked through me. "We are readers," she said, not of text but of people. Then she started telling my story.

Thirty-three-year-old woman. Nightmares of a crash. A childhood steeped in guilt for distracting her mother at the wheel. And then the part I didn't know: my mother had been trying to escape my biological father. The crash wasn't an accident. He killed her. I had been saved by my mother's boyfriend—the man I still called Dad—without knowing he wasn't my biological father.

My mouth went dry. The noise of the street drained out to a hum. The late-night ad. The matching hoodies. The way Valerie's focus sharpened like a lens. It clicked and still didn't. Braxton shut his laptop, apology sitting crooked on his face. He explained they harvested true, forgotten, or hidden stories and wove them into fiction—some too raw to name outright, some sold to writers who needed a spark. He said they should've told me first; he said I could leave.

Ethics prickled at the back of my neck. Who gets to tell a story that isn't theirs? Who profits from someone else's pain? And yet the details Valerie named fit into gaps I hadn't known were there: the recurring dream of shrieking metal and glass, the driver who died every time, the way Dad never talked about the past but poured all his hope into the future I could write.

"You can't keep it bottled up," Adrian told me. "Your story's worth telling." Maybe not for a bestseller. Maybe not even for anyone else yet. But mine, finally.

I sat there, hands empty, no notebook, no screen—just the shape of a truth I'd never had words for. For the first time, I felt a shape behind the words, a pulse. I would come back on Thursday or I wouldn't. Either way, before I could finish my novel, I had to find out where my life actually began—what was stolen, what was saved, and what I was brave enough to write down as my own.
